title: Phase Field Simulation for Dendrite Growth in Energy Storage Batteries

Condition.evidence: Wang et al.47 found that increasing the electrolyte concentration can significantly reduce dendrite size
as the overpotential increases, the dendrite growth rate accelerates significantly

lower temperatures slow down lithium-ion diffusion in the electrolyte, resulting in longer dendrites and a higher probability of dead lithium generation

as the overpotential increases, the dendrite growth rate accelerates significantly
the instability of the electrodeposition process is also intensified, leading to more pronounced dendritic branching
Most of these studies have shown that increasing temperature leads to a reduction in dendrite size25,47,51,80 and a decrease in the degree of dendritic branching
Most findings suggest that higher mechanical strength of the electrolyte and greater external stress exert a suppressive effect on dendrite growth
lower temperatures slow down lithium-ion diffusion in the electrolyte, resulting in longer dendrites and a higher probability of dead lithium generation

Phenomenon.description: Growth and morphological evolution of metal dendrites on battery electrode surfaces, analyzed as the central target of phase-field-based synthesis in the review.
Phenomenon.evidence: The phase-field method is an effective numerical simulation approach capable of accurately describing the dynamic process of dendrite growth in energy storage batteries.

Phenomenon.description: Formation of electrochemically inactive lithium during stripping/growth processes, linked to capacity loss and polarization in the review's application synthesis.
Phenomenon.evidence: The phase-field method exhibits powerful analytical capabilities in the study of dead lithium by dynamically simulating lithium stripping and dendrite growth, thereby revealing the key factors contributing to dead lithium formation.

Phenomenon.description: Interaction between cracks in solid-state electrolytes and dendrites, where crack propagation and internal stress promote dendrite penetration.
Phenomenon.evidence: excessive internal stress in solid electrolytes may induce new cracks, further promoting dendrite infiltration.

MaterialSystem.evidence: To investigate the underlying mechanisms of lithium dendrite growth and explore effective suppression strategies, many researchers have employed phase field models to simulate the dendrite growth process in lithium metal batteries.
With the rise of solid-state metal batteries, a growing number of researchers have also applied phase field modeling to solid-state lithium metal batteries.

Parameter.description: Ion concentration field used as a key variable affecting dendrite growth behavior.
Parameter.evidence: The formation of dendrites is fundamentally driven by the sustained deposition of ions, making ion concentration one of the key variables influencing dendrite growth behavior.

Parameter.description: Electrochemical overpotential acting as a primary driving force for dendrite growth in phase-field control equations.
Parameter.evidence: In phase-field models, overpotential serves as one of the key variables in the control equations, acting as the primary driving force for dendrite growth.

Parameter.description: Temperature field coupled into the phase-field framework and affecting dendrite size, branching, and dead lithium formation.
Parameter.evidence: Temperature is an important factor affecting dendrite growth.

Parameter.description: Mechanical strength of the electrolyte used to evaluate mechanical suppression of dendrite growth.
Parameter.evidence: Most findings suggest that higher mechanical strength of the electrolyte and greater external stress exert a suppressive effect on dendrite growth

Parameter.description: Applied or evolving stress affecting dendrite growth and dead lithium formation.
Parameter.evidence: Most findings suggest that higher mechanical strength of the electrolyte and greater external stress exert a suppressive effect on dendrite growth

Parameter.description: Material or interfacial anisotropy that changes dendrite growth behavior.
Parameter.evidence: Stronger anisotropy leads to faster dendrite growth.

Parameter.description: Geometric pore-size parameter of separators affecting ion concentration uniformity and electrodeposition.
Parameter.evidence: smaller pore sizes lead to more uniform lithium-ion concentrations on the electrode surface, promoting smoother electrodeposition

Parameter.description: Separator thickness influencing uniformity and overall dendrite growth rate.
Parameter.evidence: thicker separators result in more uniform dendrite growth, thereby reducing overall growth rates

Parameter.description: Residual or accumulated dead lithium formed during cycling.
Parameter.evidence: the relationship between residual dead lithium and discharge voltage/current is non-monotonic

Parameter.description: Electrochemically active lithium surface area affected by dead lithium formation.
Parameter.evidence: The presence of dead lithium directly reduces the active lithium surface area

Parameter.description: Rate of dendrite extension or propagation.
Parameter.evidence: as the overpotential increases, the dendrite growth rate accelerates significantly

Parameter.description: Degree of dendritic branching during growth.
Parameter.evidence: the instability of the electrodeposition process is also intensified, leading to more pronounced dendritic branching

Parameter.description: Characteristic dendrite size used in synthesized results on suppression.
Parameter.evidence: Wang et al.47 found that increasing the electrolyte concentration can significantly reduce dendrite size

Parameter.description: Severity of crack propagation in solid-state electrolytes.
Parameter.evidence: more severe crack propagation correlates with larger dendritic growth areas

Parameter.description: Area occupied by dendritic growth, especially in cracked solid electrolytes.
Parameter.evidence: more severe crack propagation correlates with larger dendritic growth areas
